data.content: 31 commits almost entirely "pref(FIRST FIT)" — you spent the whole week optimizing a first-fit allocator one tiny commit at a time, like refactoring via acupuncture. Two commits end with "not finish" which is both honest and a little heartbreaking. At least you opened 2 issues, so there's written evidence of the pain.

46 commits with FIVE that just say "merge(*)" — at this point you're not merging branches, you're merging your will to write proper commit messages into the void. Four "docs(README): update" in one week means you're either perfecting a README or using it as a diary. 7 PRs is impressive until you notice half the commits are merge conflict resolution.

31 commits and "working on riscv vmm" appears FIVE times — we get it, you're working on it, you don't need to narrate your journey like it's a hiking vlog. You also reverted your own "change to uppercase" commit in the same week, which is the git equivalent of loudly changing your mind at a restaurant. At least the i386 VMM actually got done between all the riscv soul-searching.

31 commits and you're simultaneously building a kernel AND reimplementing the C++ standard library — vector, list, type_traits, iostream, bitmap, string — because why use an existing libcxx when you can spend a week recreating 40 years of C++ in a hobby OS? "feat(LIBCXX): new" appearing twice tells me you started the standard library, forgot, and started it again.
